request.question: Robert Flores hosted the live afternoon edition of "SportsCenter" from noon - 3 p.m. with Chris McKendry until early September 2009, when he was replaced with a sportscaster who has been an anchor for ESPN since what year ?

title: College Football Scoreboard
Robert Flores
Jay Crawford
ESPN Classic Remembers
Chris McKendry
Dana Jacobson
Jay Harris (sportscaster)
John Buccigross
Chris Berman
Scott Van Pelt
sentences: College Football Scoreboard is a program on ESPN and ESPN2 that provides up to the minute scores and highlights during the college football season.
The official name is "College Football Scoreboard presented by Acura".
The name of the show was College "Gameday" Scoreboard up until 2006.
It airs four times a day at 3 p.m. ET and 7 p.m. ET on ESPN and at 3 p.m. ET and at 7 p.m. ET on ESPN2.
The 3 p.m. ET programs on both networks are thirty minutes long and the 7 p.m. ET programs on both networks lead up to "College Football Primetime".
However, it is subject to, and often pre-empted due to earlier games running long into the show's timeslot, and often games run into each other without any kind of "Scoreboard" interlude.
Robert Flores (born July 7, 1970) is a sports journalist, who works for MLB Network and NHL Network as a studio host for each.
Flores formerly worked at ESPN.
Joining the network in 2005, Flores was an anchor for ESPNEWS and for ESPN's "SportsCenter" (2007–2016).
Flores provided studio updates during each game of ABC College Football, and Saturday Night Football.
He also served as a substitute studio host for ESPN2's "Friday Night Fights".
Flores hosted the live afternoon edition of "SportsCenter" from noon - 3 p.m. with Chris McKendry until early September 2009, when he was replaced with John Buccigross.
He was also a substitute host for Baseball Tonight.
Flores announced on February 4, 2016 that he would be leaving ESPN after ten years.
Jason "Jay" Crawford (born July 4, 1965) is an American sports journalist who was formerly employed by ESPN.
Crawford anchored the live 11AM-1PM edition of "SportsCenter" with Chris McKendry until April 2017.

Prior to that, Crawford spent nine years hosting ESPN's morning show "Cold Pizza" and its successor "ESPN First Take", as well as "Cold Pizza"<nowiki>'</nowiki>s spinoff series "1st and 10" until its 2011 cancellation.
ESPN Classic Remembers is a television program on ESPN Classic that chronicles historic games and events, personalities and other sports milestones.
The show debuted on September 11, 2006 and is hosted by ESPN "SportsCenter" personality Chris McKendry.
It is primarily a thirty-minute program followed by a rerun of the sporting event it is chronicling.
Chris McKendry (born Christine McKendry February 18, 1968) is a journalist for ESPN, a role she has served since 1996.
She was co-anchor of the 11-1pm ET weekday block of live ESPN "SportsCenter" shows, alongside Jay Crawford.
As of April 1, 2016, she serves as full-time on-site host for ESPN tennis coverage of the Australian Open, French Open, Wimbledon and US Open.
Dana Jacobson (born November 5, 1971) is a sports anchorwoman for CBS.
She joined the network in 2013 after ten years at ESPN, where she began as an ESPNEWS anchor in December 2002 and soon became a regular anchor on the 6 p.m. edition of "SportsCenter".
In March 2005, she was named co-host of "Cold Pizza", and transitioned with the show as it became "ESPN First Take".
Jacobson provided sideline reporting for ESPN's coverage of NBA Sunday night games.
On Monday, December 26, 2011, Jacobson announced that Friday, December 30, 2011, would be her final day on "First Take."
She returned to anchoring SportsCenter shortly thereafter.
On March 27, 2012, USA Today announced that Jacobson would leave ESPN when her contract expires at the end of April.
Monday, April 30, 2012 was her final day at ESPN when she anchored the 6-8 p.m. ET SportsCenter show.
Jacobson joined CBS Sports and the cast of CBS Sports Radio's morning show.
"TBD in the AM" in January 2013, co-hosting along with Tiki Barber and Brandon Tierney.
On December 5, 2014, Dana tweeted out that she would be leaving "TBD in the AM" to work on CBS Sports Network.
Jay Harris (born February 22, 1965 in Norfolk, Virginia) is an American journalist who has worked for ESPN since February 2003.
Jay currently calls late night his home, seen primarily on the 11pm and midnight eastern editions of SportsCenter, and sometimes on the west coast Sportscenter from Los Angeles.

Jay has hosted a variety of shows during his tenure at ESPN, including Sportscenter, Outside The Lines, NFL Live, Baseball Tonight, Cold Pizza, First Take, Friday Night Fights, and ESPN Sports Saturday on ABC.
John Buccigross ( ) is an American sportscaster.
He has been an anchor for ESPN since 1996.
Christopher James Berman (born May 10, 1955), nicknamed Boomer, is an American sportscaster.
He has been an anchor for "SportsCenter" on ESPN since 1979, joining a month after its initial launch, and hosted the network's "Sunday NFL Countdown" program from 1985 to 2016.
He has also anchored "Monday Night Countdown", U.S. Open golf, the Stanley Cup Finals, and other programming on ESPN and ABC Sports.
Berman calls play-by-play of select Major League Baseball games for ESPN, which included the Home Run Derby until 2016.

A six-time honoree of the National Sports Media Association's "National Sportscaster of the Year" award, Berman was instrumental in establishing ESPN's lasting popularity during the network's formative years.
He is well known for his various catchphrases and quirky demeanor.
In January 2017, it was announced that Berman would be stepping down from several NFL-related roles at ESPN, but would be remaining at the company.
Scott Van Pelt is an American sportscaster and sports talk show host.
He co-anchored the 11 p.m. edition of "SportsCenter" on ESPN, served as the co-host of "SVP & Russillo" alongside Ryen Russillo on ESPN Radio, and hosts various golf events for the network.
In June 2015, Van Pelt left his radio show to become a solo anchor for a midnight edition of "SportsCenter".

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Produce a compact, structured first-hop summary that helps the next agent fetch the missing second-hop facts. The summary must be one labeled block with these four short sections (each 1–4 lines):

1) Evidence found — For each concrete target term or name mentioned in the passages, list the target and which passage(s) mention it, and quote a short verbatim excerpt (≤15 words) or any exact numeric/date token from the passage. Preserve the quoted text and any numeric/date formatting exactly as it appears in the passages. Include passage identifier(s) exactly as given (title or index).

2) Ambiguities — Enumerate any names or terms that correspond to multiple candidate pages or where the passages return multiple variants or conflicting values. For each ambiguous item, list the candidate labels or note the conflict using only tokens from the passages where possible.

3) Bridge / missing fact — State exactly which relation(s) or fact(s) needed to answer the question are not present or are unresolved in the provided passages (e.g., birth date, publication year, author of adaptation, founding year, award count). Be explicit and concise.

4) Retrieval direction — Provide 2–4 concrete next-hop retrievals: specific page titles or disambiguation pages, or precise search queries the next agent should run to fetch the missing bridge facts. Use actionable, precise phrasing (page title or quoted query). End this section with an explicit deferral of the final answer when the passages do not unambiguously supply all required facts.

Formatting and behavior rules:
- Produce only the single field `summary` as the output. The `summary` text must follow the four labeled sections above.
- Always quote verbatim evidence (names, short excerpts, dates, numbers) exactly as in the passages; do not reformat numeric/date tokens.
- Do NOT produce a final answer here unless every required fact to resolve the question is present and unambiguous in the provided passages (i.e., each named entity corresponds to a single candidate and the needed relation value is explicitly present). If facts are unambiguous and complete, you may state that and include a concise final conclusion in the `summary`; otherwise explicitly defer (for example: "Final answer deferred pending retrieval of the targets below.").
- If the natural summary would otherwise be a single bare token or name, append a one-line bridge clause that identifies the missing relation and at least one retrieval target rather than ending with the bare token.
- Keep phrasing concise and factual; favor short lines, source-linked evidence excerpts, and explicit next-step instructions. Do not attempt to fetch pages yourself—only list retrieval targets/queries.

Example structure to follow in the `summary` field (use passage text and titles exactly):
Evidence found: ...
Ambiguities: ...
Bridge / missing fact: ...
Retrieval direction: ...

End of instructions.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Required output format and constraints:
- Produce exactly one field named `query`. Its value must be a single short, keyword-style search string (no full sentences, no explanations, no assertions, no user-facing clarifications).
- The query must be terse (preferably <= 12 words) and contain no clause punctuation or sentence grammar (no question marks, no leading "who/what is", no verbs forming assertions).
- Use only tokens that are visible in `question` and/or `summary_1`, except you may insert at most one minimal neutral connector token to target bridging evidence (allowed connector tokens include, but are not limited to: artist, album, director, who directed, biography, introduction, genre, regular season). Do not invent new entity names or facts.
- When the inputs present multiple entities or candidate items that must be linked (for example two titles, two names, or an anchor title plus a candidate), include both entity tokens from the inputs and a single neutral connector token between or adjacent to them to indicate the evidence bridge to retrieve (e.g., "EntityA EntityB director").
- If the question or summary includes structured tokens relevant to the bridge (years, league IDs, edition names), include those tokens plus a concise cue (for example year + league + "regular season") as part of the short query.
- You may perform light normalization of obvious misspellings in entity names or technical terms drawn from the visible inputs (fix clear typos), but do not invent alternative candidate entities or assert corrected identities beyond the token-level fix.
- If it is not possible to form a fully disambiguating query from visible tokens, still emit the best compact retrieval query possible using only visible tokens and an allowed connector; do not ask the user for clarification at this step. (Downstream retrieval will be attempted first; user clarification is allowed only after retrieval fails.)

Produce only the `query` field value (the single short search string) and nothing else.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Instructions for producing `summary` (single text field only):
- Output exactly one concise summary string (no extra fields, labels, or metadata). Keep the summary to 1–2 short sentences unless the evidence forces a single-token identification (see below).
- Primary goal: integrate the first-hop identity in `context` with facts found in `passages` so the final answer agent has the explicit, supported linkage it needs.

When the question requires linking the context entity to information in the passages and the passages supply the bridging fact:
- Produce a concise (1–2 sentence) integrated summary that (1) names the context entity (using the form present in `context`), (2) names the supporting entity/attribute found in `passages` (using the form present in the inputs), and (3) states the explicit relation or fact (membership, creator attribution, partnership, founding/establishment, award, etc.) exactly as supported by the inputs. Use only facts present in `context` and `passages` and avoid introducing any unsupported details.

Name and attribution formatting:
- If a canonical multi-token name (given + family name or full official name) appears in the inputs, use that full form in the summary; do not truncate to a surname or partial name.
- For questions about creator/author/credit, prefer the person-name attribution as given in the inputs over returning the title of a work or event.

Identification requests:
- If the question explicitly asks for a single identification (for example, asks "which X" or otherwise requests a named token or label), return exactly the canonical name(s) as presented in the passages. If multiple valid names are requested, return them comma-separated. Do not add explanatory clauses, relational fragments, or extra text in these cases—only the canonical name(s).

When evidence is missing or ambiguous:
- If the passages do not supply a clear bridging fact, state that succinctly (e.g., "No supporting evidence in the provided passages" or "Unclear from the provided sources"). Retain uncertainty rather than asserting unsupported facts.

Conciseness and fidelity constraints:
- Keep the summary short and factual (1–2 sentences). Do not expand into long biographies, exhaustive lists, or unrelated details.
- Do not assert facts that are not present in `context` and `passages`.
- Avoid long verbatim copying of passage text, but short verbatim names or phrases necessary for fidelity are allowed.
- Treat the `context` field as the primary first-hop identity to be linked to `passages` when integrating evidence.

Produce the `summary` now based only on the supplied `question`, `context`, and `passages`.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Use summary_1 and summary_2 as the sole evidential basis for the final answer; give greatest weight to summary_2 (the later, post-retrieval integrated evidence). Do not introduce facts from outside these summaries.

Output rules (apply in order):

1) If summary_1 and summary_2 clearly and unambiguously agree on the same concise answer, set the value of `answer` to exactly one minimal string and nothing else:
   - For a binary yes/no conclusion, emit the single lowercase token: yes or no.
   - For any other concise label, emit that label exactly as it appears in the summaries (trim leading/trailing whitespace and strip any surrounding punctuation or wrapper syntax such as surrounding parentheses, quotes, or JSON-like braces). Do not add words, sentences, punctuation, quotes, brackets, or any other surrounding text.

2) Preserve multi-token labels: if any visible summary contains a multi-token form of the answer (for example a full personal name, a multi-word company or place), output that full multi-token form verbatim. Only shorten a label if no multi-token form appears in either summary.

3) Company/developer preference: when the question requests a company or developer and the summaries show a subordinate studio together with an explicit parent/publisher label (e.g., in parentheses or with phrasing like "published by"), choose the parent/publisher name as the single minimal answer string. If no parent/publisher is explicitly indicated, choose the best-supported developer/studio name from the summaries.

4) If the summaries conflict or are ambiguous, do NOT apply the unanimous-agreement rule in (1). Instead, use summary_2 as the primary basis and emit the minimal concise string supported by summary_2, applying rules (2) and (3) as relevant. If summary_2 provides no concise supported answer, set `answer` to the minimal explicit token "unknown".

Formatting constraints (must always be followed):
- Produce exactly one field: `answer`.
- The value of `answer` must be a single minimal string (no surrounding text, punctuation, explanation, or structural wrappers).
- Do not output extra fields, labels, or markup.

Additional guidance:
- If summary_1 describes an earlier unresolved bridge but summary_2 provides a resolved, supported conclusion, use the conclusion from summary_2.
- If a concise answer string can be drawn verbatim from the summaries, prefer that verbatim text over paraphrase.

End.

response.hop1_query: Robert Flores hosted the live afternoon edition of "SportsCenter" from noon - 3 p.m. with Chris McKendry until early September 2009, when he was replaced with a sportscaster who has been an anchor for ESPN since what year ?
hop1_docs: Robert Flores | Robert Flores (born July 7, 1970) is a sports journalist, who works for MLB Network and NHL Network as a studio host for each.  Flores formerly worked at ESPN.  Joining the network in 2005, Flores was an anchor for ESPNEWS and for ESPN's "SportsCenter" (2007–2016).  Flores provided studio updates during each game of ABC College Football, and Saturday Night Football.  He also served as a substitute studio host for ESPN2's "Friday Night Fights".  Flores hosted the live afternoon edition of "SportsCenter" from noon - 3 p.m. with Chris McKendry until early September 2009, when he was replaced with John Buccigross.  He was also a substitute host for Baseball Tonight.  Flores announced on February 4, 2016 that he would be leaving ESPN after ten years.

Chris McKendry | Chris McKendry (born Christine McKendry February 18, 1968) is a journalist for ESPN, a role she has served since 1996.  She was co-anchor of the 11-1pm ET weekday block of live ESPN "SportsCenter" shows, alongside Jay Crawford.  As of April 1, 2016, she serves as full-time on-site host for ESPN tennis coverage of the Australian Open, French Open, Wimbledon and US Open.

Jay Crawford | Jason "Jay" Crawford (born July 4, 1965) is an American sports journalist who was formerly employed by ESPN.  Crawford anchored the live 11AM-1PM edition of "SportsCenter" with Chris McKendry until April 2017.  Prior to that, Crawford spent nine years hosting ESPN's morning show "Cold Pizza" and its successor "ESPN First Take", as well as "Cold Pizza"<nowiki>'</nowiki>s spinoff series "1st and 10" until its 2011 cancellation.

Chris Berman | Christopher James Berman (born May 10, 1955), nicknamed Boomer, is an American sportscaster.  He has been an anchor for "SportsCenter" on ESPN since 1979, joining a month after its initial launch, and hosted the network's "Sunday NFL Countdown" program from 1985 to 2016.  He has also anchored "Monday Night Countdown", U.S. Open golf, the Stanley Cup Finals, and other programming on ESPN and ABC Sports.  Berman calls play-by-play of select Major League Baseball games for ESPN, which included the Home Run Derby until 2016.  A six-time honoree of the National Sports Media Association's "National Sportscaster of the Year" award, Berman was instrumental in establishing ESPN's lasting popularity during the network's formative years.  He is well known for his various catchphrases and quirky demeanor.  In January 2017, it was announced that Berman would be stepping down from several NFL-related roles at ESPN, but would be remaining at the company.

Scott Van Pelt | Scott Van Pelt is an American sportscaster and sports talk show host.  He co-anchored the 11 p.m. edition of "SportsCenter" on ESPN, served as the co-host of "SVP & Russillo" alongside Ryen Russillo on ESPN Radio, and hosts various golf events for the network.  In June 2015, Van Pelt left his radio show to become a solo anchor for a midnight edition of "SportsCenter".

Jay Harris (sportscaster) | Jay Harris (born February 22, 1965 in Norfolk, Virginia) is an American journalist who has worked for ESPN since February 2003.  Jay currently calls late night his home, seen primarily on the 11pm and midnight eastern editions of SportsCenter, and sometimes on the west coast Sportscenter from Los Angeles.  Jay has hosted a variety of shows during his tenure at ESPN, including Sportscenter, Outside The Lines, NFL Live, Baseball Tonight, Cold Pizza, First Take, Friday Night Fights, and ESPN Sports Saturday on ABC.

Sara Walsh | Sara Elizabeth Walsh (born April 12, 1978) is an American sportscaster who joined ESPN in May 2010.  Walsh came to ESPN from WUSA-TV in Washington, D.C., where she served as the station’s weekend sports anchor and Redskins beat reporter from 2006 to 2010.  Prior to WUSA, Walsh worked at WKRN in Nashville from 2003 to 2006, winning four regional Emmys in three years.  She co-hosted the weekly “Monday Night Live” with Titans Coach Jeff Fisher, and hosted a weekly radio show with then Titans defensive tackle Albert Haynesworth.  Walsh also served as Sports Director at WPGA in Macon, Georgia from 2001 to 2003, and began her career as a sports writer for the "Beaches Leader" newspaper in Jacksonville Beach.  She was most recently an anchor on ESPN's "SportsCenter" until May 4, 2017, when she was let go by the network.
response.summary_1:
Evidence found:
- Robert Flores — [1] "Robert Flores (born July 7, 1970)"
- Flores hosting detail — [1] "hosted the live afternoon edition of \"SportsCenter\" from noon - 3 p.m."
- replacement name John Buccigross — [1] "replaced with John Buccigross."
- Chris McKendry — [1] "with Chris McKendry until early September 2009"
- Chris McKendry anchor-tenure token — [2] "since 1996."
- Jay Crawford — [3] "Crawford anchored the live 11AM-1PM edition of \"SportsCenter\""
- Chris Berman — [4] "has been an anchor for \"SportsCenter\" on ESPN since 1979"
- Scott Van Pelt — [5] "co-anchored the 11 p.m. edition of \"SportsCenter\""
- Jay Harris — [6] "has worked for ESPN since February 2003."
- Sara Walsh — [7] "joined ESPN in May 2010."

Ambiguities:
- None of the passages give any year for John Buccigross; no conflicting "anchor since" years are present for him.
- Multiple other anchors have explicit "since" years (Chris Berman 1979; Chris McKendry 1996; Jay Harris February 2003), but they are not the replacement named in [1].

Bridge / missing fact:
- Missing: the year John Buccigross "has been an anchor for ESPN since" (the exact "anchor since" year for John Buccigross is not stated in the provided passages).

Retrieval direction:
1) Fetch the page "John Buccigross" (Wikipedia) — check lead/bio for "has been an anchor for ESPN since" year.
2) Query: "John Buccigross ESPN anchor since" (search engine) to locate biographical summaries stating the anchor-start year.
3) Query: "John Buccigross biography 'anchor for ESPN since' site:espn.com" to find ESPN's official bio or press pages.
Final answer deferred pending retrieval of the targets above.
hop2_docs: John Buccigross | John Buccigross ( ) is an American sportscaster.  He has been an anchor for ESPN since 1996.

Robert Flores | Robert Flores (born July 7, 1970) is a sports journalist, who works for MLB Network and NHL Network as a studio host for each.  Flores formerly worked at ESPN.  Joining the network in 2005, Flores was an anchor for ESPNEWS and for ESPN's "SportsCenter" (2007–2016).  Flores provided studio updates during each game of ABC College Football, and Saturday Night Football.  He also served as a substitute studio host for ESPN2's "Friday Night Fights".  Flores hosted the live afternoon edition of "SportsCenter" from noon - 3 p.m. with Chris McKendry until early September 2009, when he was replaced with John Buccigross.  He was also a substitute host for Baseball Tonight.  Flores announced on February 4, 2016 that he would be leaving ESPN after ten years.

John Muller | John William Muller (born July 7, 1966) is an American television journalist.  Since 2011, he has been with ABC News, where his anchor duties include "World News Now" and "ABC News Now".  He is the weeknight anchor of NYC's Emmy Award-winning "PIX 11 Morning News".  He worked as a reporter and anchor in Georgia and Florida before returning to New York City.
John McCaa | John McCaa (born in Rantoul, Illinois) is a news anchor currently working for WFAA-TV in Dallas, Texas.  He has been working for WFAA since 1984.
John Beard (news anchor) | John Beard (born 1948) is an American news anchor based in Buffalo, NY.

John King (journalist) | John King (born August 30, 1963) is an American news anchor.  He is CNN's chief national correspondent, based in Washington, DC, and is anchor of its roundtable political discussion program "Inside Politics".  He is the former anchor of "State of the Union", as well as "John King, USA", which appeared weeknights at 7pm/ET on CNN before being cancelled.

John Seigenthaler (anchorman) | John Michael Seigenthaler (born December 21, 1955) is an American news anchor.  He is well known as a former weekend anchor and correspondent for both NBC and MSNBC.  He is the son of the late newspaper journalist John Lawrence Seigenthaler, and named for his father.  He is best known for his eight-year tenure as weekend anchor of NBC Nightly News.  In July 2008, Seigenthaler joined his family company Seigenthaler Public Relations, but continued to be based in New York.  From 2013-2016 he worked for the now defunct Al Jazeera America as its evening news anchor.
response.summary_2: Robert Flores was replaced by John Buccigross, who has been an anchor for ESPN since 1996.
